text:
Flat Barbell Bench Press — Setup and Body Position

Back arch: A natural arch in the lumbar spine is both safe and mechanically advantageous — it shortens the range of motion and stabilizes the shoulder girdle. Extreme competition-style arches (butt nearly off the bench) are only necessary in powerlifting; for general training a moderate natural arch is appropriate. The butt must remain in contact with the bench.

Foot placement: Feet flat on the floor provide the most stability and allow leg drive — a technique used to transfer force from the lower body through the torso into the press. Feet should be pulled back toward the hips, not stretched forward. If flat feet cannot be achieved (short limbs, high bench), place feet on plates — never tuck feet on the bench.

Scapular retraction and depression: Pull the shoulder blades together and down (retract and depress) before unracking. This creates a solid base of support, protects the anterior capsule of the shoulder, and keeps the shoulders out of an impingement-prone position. Maintain this throughout every rep.

Grip width: Slightly wider than shoulder-width — typically a 1–2 finger-width inside the knurling rings for most athletes. Wider grip reduces range of motion and increases pec recruitment; narrower grip (closer to shoulder-width) shifts emphasis to triceps. The forearms should be perpendicular to the floor at the bottom position when viewed from the side.

Wrist position: Keep wrists stacked directly over the elbows — neutral, not extended. A cocked wrist dramatically increases wrist joint stress and shifts the bar out of a straight vertical line over the forearm.

Flat Barbell Bench Press — Bar Path, Touch Point, and Common Errors

Unracking: Take the bar out with fully locked elbows and move it directly over the lower chest before beginning the set. Do not start the descent from over the face or throat.

Bar path: Not straight down — the bar travels in a slight J-curve, touching the lower chest (just above the xiphoid process) and pressing back to a point slightly above the starting position (over the mid-chest). This J-curve keeps the bar over the mid-foot of the forearm-elbow axis throughout.

Elbow angle: 45–75 degrees from the torso is the range that balances pec recruitment and shoulder safety. Elbows at 90 degrees (flared perpendicular to the torso) maximally stresses the anterior capsule of the shoulder and is the cause of most bench-related shoulder injuries. Elbows tucked too close (less than 45 degrees) shifts the load predominantly to the triceps and reduces pec contribution.

Touch: The bar should touch the chest (or come within an inch of it) on every rep — bouncing off the chest is cheating; stopping 3 inches short removes tension at the bottom and the stretch-shortening reflex benefit.

Common errors:
1. Shoulder blades unlocking — the scap platform collapses on descent, causing the shoulders to round forward. Fix: 'break the bar apart' cue keeps the lats engaged.
2. Bar drifting toward the face on the press — usually caused by taking the descent too far up the chest. Fix: keep touch point at lower chest.
3. Butt rising off the bench — indicates excessive arch or load beyond technical capacity. Fix: reduce load, focus on maintaining glute contact.
4. Uneven press — one side leading. Fix: pause reps, lighter loads, address shoulder mobility asymmetry.
